extracted_information: Docker's security architecture is built upon four main areas: the intrinsic security of the Linux kernel's namespaces and cgroups, the attack surface of the Docker daemon, container configuration profile loopholes, and kernel hardening features. Docker containers leverage kernel-level isolation mechanisms like namespaces for process and network segregation, and control groups (cgroups) for resource management and denial-of-service prevention. The Docker daemon, often requiring root privileges (though Rootless mode exists), necessitates careful access control. Docker significantly enhances container security by operating with a restricted set of Linux capabilities, reducing the privileges of 'root' within a container. Additional security measures include Docker Content Trust for signed images, integration with other kernel security features like AppArmor and SELinux, and support for User Namespaces to map container root to a non-root host user.

specifications.docker_daemon_api_change: Docker 0.5.2 (Unix socket instead of TCP socket)
specifications.default_capabilities_dropped_by_docker: All capabilities except those explicitly needed (allowlist approach)
example_deniable_capabilities: certain filesystem operations (creating device nodes, changing owner, altering attributes)

features.name: Kernel Namespaces
features.description: Provide the primary form of isolation by segregating processes, file systems, and network stacks. Processes in one container cannot see or affect processes in another, or on the host. Each container gets its own network stack.
features.details: Similar to LXC containers. Allows inter-container network traffic if public ports are specified or links are used. All containers on a Docker host are on bridge interfaces.

features.name: Control Groups (cgroups)
features.description: Implement resource accounting and limiting (memory, CPU, disk I/O). Prevents a single container from exhausting system resources and helps fend off denial-of-service attacks.
features.details: Essential for multi-tenant platforms to guarantee consistent uptime and performance.

features.name: Docker Daemon Attack Surface Management
features.description: Addresses security concerns related to the Docker daemon, which typically requires root privileges.
features.details: Only trusted users should control the daemon. Implications of directory sharing (e.g., sharing '/' directory allows container to alter host filesystem). REST API (DOCKER_HOST) should use Unix sockets or be secured with HTTPS/certificates and restricted to trusted networks/VPNs. Image loading/pulling extracted in chrooted subprocess (1.3.2+) and accessed via cryptographic checksums (1.10.0+).

features.name: Linux Kernel Capabilities
features.description: Transforms the 'root/non-root' dichotomy into a fine-grained access control system. Docker defaults to a restricted set of capabilities for containers, reducing the privileges of 'root' inside a container.
features.details: By default, Docker drops all capabilities except those explicitly needed (allowlist). Allows granular control over permissions (e.g., `net_bind_service`). Reduces attack vectors, making it harder for an intruder to cause serious damage or escalate to the host even if root is compromised within a container.

features.name: Docker Content Trust
features.description: Enables Docker Engine to run only cryptographically signed images.
features.details: Configured via `daemon.json` with trustpinning, requiring repositories to be signed with a user-specified root key. Provides insight and enforcement for image signature verification.

features.name: Other Kernel Security Features Integration
features.description: Compatibility with existing Linux kernel security mechanisms to harden the Docker host and containers.
features.details: Does not interfere with systems like TOMOYO, AppArmor, SELinux, GRSEC, PAX. Allows use of distribution-provided security model templates (e.g., AppArmor, SELinux policies). Users can define custom policies.

features.name: User Namespaces
features.description: Maps the root user inside a container to a non-uid-0 user outside the container.
features.details: Supported directly by the Docker daemon since 1.10. Not enabled by default. Mitigates risks of container breakout.

temporal_info.kernel_namespaces_maturity: Introduced between 2.6.15 and 2.6.26 (July 2008), design inspired by OpenVZ (2005)
temporal_info.control_groups_maturity: Code started 2006, initially merged in kernel 2.6.24
